title: Hormones, pheromones and behaviour
summary: Chemical messengers shape what we do. This lesson shows how hormones — cortisol, oxytocin and adrenaline — change human behaviour such as stress, trust and memory, and then weighs the far shakier evidence for pheromones in humans. Every claim is anchored to a named study (aim, procedure, findings) and explicitly linked to behaviour, then evaluated for its methodological and ethical limits — exactly the skill a Paper 1 SAQ rewards.
learningObjectives: Explain the effect of at least one hormone on human behaviour, using a named study with its aim, procedure and findings.
Describe the debated role of pheromones in human behaviour and explain why the evidence is regarded as mixed and inconclusive.
Distinguish clearly between hormones, neurotransmitters and pheromones.
Evaluate research on hormones and pheromones, identifying methodological and ethical issues.
Describe a study AND explicitly link it to the behaviour in the question — the move that separates the top SAQ band from the middle.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: The Body's Chemical Messengers
simpleExplanation.summary: Hormones are chemical signals released by glands into your bloodstream. They travel to distant target organs and change how you feel and act — how you handle stress, how well you remember, how much you trust others. Pheromones are chemical signals released outside the body that are supposed to influence other people, but the evidence that they actually do this in humans is weak and disputed.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture your body as a company. Hormones are internal memos couriered through the bloodstream to specific departments (target organs) telling them what to do — slowly, but with lasting effect. Pheromones would be more like a scent sprayed into the shared office air, hoping to shift the mood of whoever breathes it. In insects that 'scent' works reliably; in humans we are not even sure the memo is ever read.
steps: Name the chemical and classify it correctly — a hormone (e.g. cortisol, oxytocin, adrenaline) or a PUTATIVE (unproven) pheromone (e.g. androstadienone).
Describe how it works: released from a gland into the bloodstream for a hormone; hypothesised airborne detection for a pheromone.
State the specific behaviour it is claimed to affect — e.g. adrenaline enhancing emotional memory, oxytocin increasing trust.
Support the claim with a named study (aim, procedure, findings) and, crucially, LINK that study back to the behaviour in the question.

flashcards.back: A chemical messenger secreted by an endocrine gland directly into the bloodstream, travelling to distant target cells with matching receptors. Effects are slower to start than nerve impulses but longer-lasting.

A chemical released by a neuron across a synapse to an adjacent cell — fast and localised. Hormones travel via blood and act body-wide. Some chemicals (e.g. adrenaline, noradrenaline) can act as BOTH depending on context, which is why precision matters.

A chemical released by an individual into the environment that affects the physiology or behaviour of OTHER members of the same species. Well established in insects and some mammals; its existence and effect in humans is controversial.
The network of glands (adrenal, pituitary, etc.) that produce hormones and secrete them into the circulatory system to be carried to target organs.

A steroid 'stress hormone' released by the adrenal glands via the HPA axis. Regulates metabolism and immune response, and modulates memory — moderate levels can aid it, while prolonged high levels can impair the retrieval of declarative memories.
A hormone produced in the hypothalamus and released by the pituitary. Associated with bonding, social approach and — in some studies — increased interpersonal trust.
A hormone released by the adrenal glands during stress or excitement, driving the 'fight or flight' response and enhancing the consolidation of emotionally arousing memories.
A steroid in male sweat studied as a PUTATIVE (potential, unproven) human pheromone that may modulate women's mood — not a proven aphrodisiac.
A sensory organ that detects pheromones in many mammals. In adult humans it appears vestigial (non-functional), a key anatomical argument against functional human pheromones.

Aim: test cortisol's effect on memory. Procedure: participants took a low-dose, high-dose or placebo cortisol tablet for days, then did a verbal recall task. Findings: the high-dose group recalled worse — evidence that raised cortisol impairs declarative memory. LINK: shows a hormone changing the behaviour of remembering.

Aim: test oxytocin's effect on trust. Procedure: double-blind; participants inhaled oxytocin or placebo, then played a trust game with feedback about betrayal, in an fMRI scanner. Findings: after betrayal, placebo players reduced trust but oxytocin players kept investing, with lower amygdala activity. LINK: a hormone changing trusting behaviour.

Aim: test adrenaline's role in emotional memory. Procedure: groups heard an emotional vs neutral story; a follow-up gave some participants a beta-blocker (propranolol) that blocks adrenaline. Findings: the emotional group recalled more, but the beta-blocker removed that advantage. LINK: adrenaline enhances the consolidation of emotional memory.

flashcards.front: Trap: hormone vs neurotransmitter vs pheromone
flashcards.back: Hormone = bloodstream, within one body. Neurotransmitter = synapse, within the nervous system. Pheromone = released OUTSIDE the body to affect ANOTHER individual. Mislabelling the chemical undermines the whole answer.

Human pheromone research is inconclusive. Use cautious language ('putative pheromone', 'research suggests') and be ready to critique flaws (unnatural doses, confounds). Never write 'pheromones cause attraction' as established fact.

A study showing high cortisol alongside poor memory in stressed people is CORRELATIONAL. Only a controlled experiment that manipulates the hormone (e.g. Newcomer's dosing) lets you claim the hormone caused the behaviour change.

quickCheck.prompt: A chemical that is released OUTSIDE the body and is claimed to affect the behaviour of ANOTHER person is best described as a:
Which statement most accurately reflects the current scientific view of pheromones in humans?
options: Human pheromones are proven to cause sexual attraction
The evidence is mixed and inconclusive, and effects (if any) are subtle
Humans have no chemical signalling of any kind
The vomeronasal organ makes human pheromones certain
quickCheck.answer: The evidence is mixed and inconclusive, and effects (if any) are subtle

quickCheck.prompt: A student writes: 'Baumgartner (2008) — oxytocin — trust game — fMRI.' In SAQ marking terms, this counts as:
options: A study that is DESCRIBED and linked
A study that is only LISTED, not described
quickCheck.answer: A study that is only LISTED, not described

quickCheck.prompt: A survey finds people with higher cortisol tend to report worse memory. Why can't this alone show cortisol impairs memory?
options: It is correlational — a third variable or reverse causation could explain it
The sample is always too large
quickCheck.answer: It is correlational — a third variable or reverse causation could explain it

quickCheck.prompt: Which chemical can act as BOTH a hormone (in the blood) and a neurotransmitter (in the nervous system), so must be described in context?

faq.q: What is the difference between a hormone, a neurotransmitter and a pheromone?
faq.a: A hormone is secreted by a gland into the bloodstream and acts on distant target cells inside the SAME body — slow but long-lasting. A neurotransmitter is released across a synapse to an adjacent cell within the nervous system — fast and localised. A pheromone is released OUTSIDE the body into the environment and is supposed to affect ANOTHER individual of the same species. Getting this classification right is the first thing an examiner checks: calling adrenaline a pheromone, or oxytocin a neurotransmitter without qualification, weakens the whole answer.

faq.q: Do humans really have working pheromones?
faq.a: This is genuinely unsettled. Pheromones are well established in insects and some mammals, but the human evidence is weak, inconsistent and disputed. The vomeronasal organ that detects pheromones in other mammals appears vestigial in adult humans, and many studies use unnaturally high chemical concentrations or fail to control social cues. The safe, mark-earning position is that IF human pheromones exist, any effect is subtle and easily overridden by cognitive and social factors. Write with cautious language, not certainty.

faq.q: For an SAQ, is it enough to just name a study?
faq.a: No — and this is the single most common reason strong-looking answers lose marks. Naming or listing a study ('McGaugh & Cahill 1995 — adrenaline — memory') sits in the bottom band. You must DESCRIBE it (aim, procedure, findings) and then EXPLICITLY LINK it back to the behaviour in the question. The link is the sentence that begins something like 'This shows that the hormone... changed the behaviour of... because...'. Description without a link caps you in the middle band; description plus an explicit link is what reaches 7–9.

faq.q: How much evaluation does a 9-mark SAQ need?
faq.a: A Paper 1 SAQ rewards focused explanation more than lengthy critique, but a sentence or two of evaluation — noting a methodological or ethical limitation such as a confounding variable, low ecological validity or a correlational design — strengthens the 'accurate knowledge' the top band asks for. Save extended evaluation for the longer ERQ; here, keep it tight and always in service of the link between hormone and behaviour.

sections.content: The biological approach argues that our physiology shapes our psychology. This lesson looks at two kinds of chemical messenger. Hormones are the body's internal communicators: released by glands into the bloodstream, they change behaviours such as how we handle stress (cortisol), how much we trust others (oxytocin) and how vividly we remember emotional events (adrenaline). Pheromones would be external messengers passing between people — but as we will see, the evidence that they actually influence human behaviour is mixed and inconclusive. Throughout, the goal is not just to know the facts but to do what a Paper 1 SAQ rewards: describe a named study and explicitly link it to behaviour.

Hormones are chemical messengers produced by the glands of the endocrine system and secreted into the bloodstream, which carries them to target cells that have matching receptors. Unlike neurotransmitters — which act almost instantly across a synapse over a tiny distance — hormones act more slowly but their effects last longer and reach the whole body. A key nuance examiners like to see: hormones do not simply CAUSE a behaviour like a switch; they raise the PROBABILITY that a behaviour occurs in response to a stimulus. That framing keeps you clear of crude biological determinism.

items: Hormones are secreted by endocrine glands (e.g. adrenal glands, pituitary, hypothalamus).
They travel through the bloodstream to reach distant target cells with specific receptors.
Effects begin more slowly than nerve impulses but are more prolonged and body-wide.
They modulate behaviour — increasing its probability — rather than mechanically causing it.

sections.content: Cortisol is a steroid hormone released by the adrenal glands through the hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal (HPA) axis when we face a stressor. In the short term it mobilises energy, but its relationship with memory is dose-dependent: moderate, brief rises can sharpen memory, whereas prolonged or high levels can impair the retrieval of declarative memories. This is why chronic stress is linked with forgetfulness and difficulty recalling learned material.

Newcomer et al. (1999) tested this experimentally. AIM: to investigate the effect of raised cortisol on verbal declarative memory. PROCEDURE: in a controlled design, participants took tablets over several days — a high dose of cortisol (roughly the level of a major stressor), a low dose, or a placebo — then completed a verbal recall task. FINDINGS: the high-dose group performed significantly worse at recall than the low-dose and placebo groups, and their memory returned to normal after the treatment stopped. LINK TO BEHAVIOUR: because the design manipulated cortisol directly, it supports a causal claim that this hormone impairs the behaviour of remembering — not merely that stress and forgetfulness happen to co-occur.

Oxytocin is produced in the hypothalamus and released by the pituitary gland. It is associated with social bonding, and several studies suggest it increases interpersonal trust. Baumgartner et al. (2008) offers a strong worked case. AIM: to investigate oxytocin's effect on trust after a person's trust had been betrayed. PROCEDURE: in a double-blind design, participants inhaled either oxytocin or a placebo and then played a 'trust game' — investing money with a trustee — while receiving feedback that they had sometimes been betrayed, all inside an fMRI scanner. FINDINGS: after betrayal, placebo participants invested less (they became wary), but oxytocin participants kept investing at the same level, and showed reduced activity in the amygdala (a fear-processing region) and feedback-related brain areas. LINK TO BEHAVIOUR: this shows the hormone oxytocin changed trusting behaviour — participants who received it were less deterred by betrayal — with a plausible neural mechanism (dampened threat response).

Adrenaline (epinephrine) is released by the adrenal glands during stress or excitement, driving the fight-or-flight response. Beyond immediate survival, the arousal it produces helps CONSOLIDATE memories, making emotionally significant events more vivid and durable than neutral ones — the biological basis often invoked for 'flashbulb memories'.

sections.question: Worked example 1 — Explain the effect of one hormone on human behaviour, with reference to one study. [9 marks]
sections.solution:
**Hormone and behaviour.** One hormone that affects human behaviour is adrenaline, which enhances the consolidation of emotional memories. Adrenaline is secreted by the adrenal glands during emotionally arousing situations; the physiological arousal it produces is thought to strengthen how strongly an experience is stored, so emotional events are remembered more vividly than neutral ones.

**Supporting study — McGaugh & Cahill (1995).**
- *Aim:* to investigate the role of adrenaline in forming emotional memories.
- *Procedure:* participants were split into two groups. One heard a neutral, mundane story about a boy and his mother visiting the father at a hospital; the other heard a version with the same opening but including an emotionally traumatic event (the boy in a serious accident). Two weeks later their memory for story details was tested. In a follow-up, participants who heard the emotional story were given either a placebo or the beta-blocker propranolol, which blocks adrenaline's receptor sites.
- *Findings:* the emotional-story group recalled significantly more details than the neutral group. Crucially, participants given propranolol recalled no better than the neutral group — blocking adrenaline removed the memory advantage.

**Explicit link.** This shows that the hormone adrenaline directly affects the human behaviour of memory: when adrenaline was allowed to act, emotional arousal enhanced recall, and when its effect was chemically blocked, that enhancement disappeared. The manipulation and its reversal make the hormone the cause of the change in remembering, which is exactly what 'explain the effect of one hormone on behaviour' requires.

**Brief evaluation.** The controlled, experimental design with a beta-blocker allows a causal inference, a strength. A limitation is ecological validity — recalling a lab story differs from real-world emotional memory — but this does not undermine the demonstrated causal role of adrenaline.

sections.content: Structure a 9-mark SAQ in three moves: (1) name the hormone and state the behaviour it affects; (2) DESCRIBE one study with its aim, procedure and findings; (3) write an explicit LINK sentence that ties the study back to the hormone-and-behaviour claim in the question. A short evaluative sentence (a confound, low ecological validity, or a correlational design) reinforces 'accurate knowledge'. The link, not the length, is what lifts you into the top band.

Pheromones are chemical signals released by one individual into the environment that affect the behaviour or physiology of others of the same species. In insects and some mammals this signalling is well documented. In humans it is genuinely contested. A central anatomical problem is that the vomeronasal organ (VNO) — which detects pheromones in other mammals — appears vestigial (non-functional) in adult humans. Even so, researchers study 'putative' (potential, unproven) human pheromones such as androstadienone (AND), found in male sweat. The evidence remains mixed and inconclusive: results are inconsistent across studies and are often confounded by social cues or produced with unnaturally high chemical doses.

sections.question: Worked example 2 — Explain, with reference to one study, the debated role of pheromones in human behaviour. [9 marks]
sections.solution:
**Concept and behaviour.** Pheromones are chemicals released outside the body that are claimed to influence others of the same species — in humans, potentially affecting mood or social perception. Because their role in humans is disputed, an accurate answer treats these as PUTATIVE pheromones and frames the evidence as mixed.

**Supporting study — Lundström & Olsson (2005).**
- *Aim:* to investigate whether androstadienone (AND), a putative pheromone in male sweat, affects women's mood.
- *Procedure:* female participants were exposed to either AND or a control solution and then interacted with either a male or a female experimenter; mood was measured by questionnaire.
- *Findings:* AND increased women's positive mood, but ONLY when the experimenter was male. The researchers concluded AND is not a simple aphrodisiac but may modulate mood in the presence of men.

**Explicit link.** This shows that a putative pheromone was associated with a change in the behaviour of self-reported mood — but only in a specific social context, which is why researchers describe the human pheromone effect as context-dependent rather than a reliable, automatic response.

**Evaluation — why the evidence is called inconclusive.** The concentration of AND used far exceeded natural levels, lowering ecological validity. The male experimenter is a confounding variable: the mood lift could stem from social interaction rather than the chemical. Participants may also have guessed the aim, producing demand characteristics. Attributing a complex emotion to one chemical is reductionist. Taken together, these limitations are exactly why psychologists treat human pheromone effects as mixed and inconclusive rather than established.

items: **Mislabelling the chemical** — calling adrenaline a pheromone, or oxytocin a neurotransmitter without qualification. Hormone = bloodstream within one body; neurotransmitter = synapse; pheromone = released outside the body to affect another person.
**Overstating pheromone evidence** — writing 'pheromones cause attraction' as fact. The human evidence is mixed and inconclusive; use 'putative pheromone' and cautious language.
**Listing a study instead of describing it** — 'McGaugh & Cahill 1995, adrenaline, memory' is only a LIST and sits in the bottom band. Give the aim, procedure and findings.

**Describing a study but never linking it** — a full description with no explicit link to the behaviour caps you in the middle band (4–6). Always add the 'this shows the hormone changed the behaviour because…' sentence.

**Confusing correlation with causation** — a study where high cortisol merely co-occurs with poor memory cannot show cortisol CAUSED it. Only a design that manipulates the hormone (e.g. Newcomer's dosing) supports a causal claim.
**Crude determinism** — saying a hormone simply 'causes' a behaviour. Hormones raise the PROBABILITY of a behaviour; cognitive and social factors still matter.

sections.content: Model answer — marked against the real IB markband

sections.question: Paper 1 SAQ: Explain the effect of one hormone on human behaviour. [9 marks]
sections.solution:
**Model answer:**
One hormone that affects human behaviour is oxytocin, which influences interpersonal trust. Oxytocin is produced in the hypothalamus and released by the pituitary gland into the bloodstream, and is associated with social bonding and approach behaviour. Research suggests it makes people more willing to trust others even after that trust has been broken.

This effect was demonstrated by Baumgartner et al. (2008). The AIM was to investigate the effect of oxytocin on trust following betrayal. In the PROCEDURE, a double-blind design was used: participants inhaled either oxytocin or a placebo and then played a 'trust game', investing money with a trustee while receiving feedback that they had sometimes been betrayed, all while their brains were scanned using fMRI. The FINDINGS were that after betrayal, participants in the placebo group invested less money — they became cautious — whereas participants in the oxytocin group continued to invest at the same level, and showed reduced activity in the amygdala and in feedback-processing brain regions.

This EXPLICITLY shows that the hormone oxytocin affected the behaviour of trusting: participants who received oxytocin kept trusting despite betrayal, while those without it withdrew their trust, and the reduced amygdala activity suggests oxytocin dampened the threat response that would normally make a betrayed person wary. A brief evaluative point: because the hormone was directly manipulated in a controlled, double-blind design, a causal link between oxytocin and trusting behaviour can reasonably be inferred, though the artificial trust-game task limits ecological validity.

---
**How this maps to our IB markband (Paper 1 SAQ, out of 9):**
- **7–9:** the response is fully focused on the question and meets the command term; knowledge is accurate and addresses the main problem; relevant research is DESCRIBED AND EXPLICITLY LINKED to the question.
- **4–6:** the response is relevant but does not meet the command term (e.g. it describes rather than explains); knowledge is limited or partial; research is described but not clearly linked.
- **1–3:** the response has limited relevance or merely rephrases the question; knowledge is inaccurate or irrelevant; research is not relevant to the question or is only LISTED.

**Why this answer reaches 7–9:** it stays fully focused on 'the effect of one hormone on behaviour'; it meets the command term 'explain' by giving a mechanism (bloodstream release, reduced amygdala threat response) rather than just a description; the knowledge is accurate; and — the decisive move — the study is not only DESCRIBED (aim, procedure, findings) but EXPLICITLY LINKED: the sentence 'this explicitly shows that the hormone oxytocin affected the behaviour of trusting… while those without it withdrew their trust' ties the evidence directly to the question. An answer that gave the same study description but stopped before that link would fall to 4–6; an answer that only named the study ('Baumgartner 2008, oxytocin, trust') would sit at 1–3.

sections.content: This is exactly what the Practice button does: write your own answer to 'Explain the effect of one hormone on human behaviour [9]', submit it, and our marking engine grades it against this same /9 SAQ markband, telling you which band you reached and why. The habit that moves you up the bands is simple to state and easy to forget under pressure: DESCRIBE the study (aim, procedure, findings) AND then LINK it explicitly to the behaviour in the question.

Hormones and pheromones are one strand of the biological approach; they sit alongside neurotransmitters, brain localisation and genetics. The exam skill you have practised here — describe a named study, then link it explicitly to behaviour, and evaluate its methodology and ethics — is the same skill every Paper 1 answer in this approach demands. Master the link, and the rest of the unit becomes a matter of swapping in the right study.
